heroOverview: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis is chronic kidney damage resulting from sustained elevation in blood pressure, characterized pathologically by arteriolar hyalinosis, arterial intimal thickening, glomerulosclerosis, and tubular atrophy. It is the second leading attributed cause of end-stage renal disease in the United States after diabetes, accounting for 27-29% of ESRD incident cases annually (USRDS 2022). Black Americans bear a disproportionate burden — ESRD from attributed hypertension is 3-4 times more frequent in Black patients than White patients, partly driven by APOL1 high-risk variants. Blood pressure control to below 130/80 mmHg with renin-angiotensin blockade is the cornerstone of management, slowing but rarely reversing established damage.

keyStats.prevalence: Attributed hypertensive ESRD accounts for 27-29% of new ESRD cases in the US annually (~25,000-28,000 incident cases per year); prevalence of CKD attributed to hypertension is estimated at 2-3 million adults in the US (USRDS 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Black Americans have 3-4x higher ESRD rate from hypertensive nephrosclerosis compared to White Americans; risk is strongly amplified by APOL1 high-risk G1/G2 alleles
keyStats.avgAge: Typically presents as CKD in adults over 50 with longstanding hypertension; ESRD from hypertension often occurs in the sixth and seventh decades
keyStats.globalCases: Hypertension-attributed CKD is among the top three causes of ESRD in most high-income countries; worldwide burden parallels global hypertension prevalence affecting 1.28 billion adults (WHO 2023)

definition:
Hypertensive nephrosclerosis (ICD-10: I12.9, also coded N26.2) refers to the structural and functional kidney damage caused by chronically elevated arterial blood pressure. The term encompasses both benign nephrosclerosis, in which gradual arteriolar hyalinosis and glomerulosclerosis develop over decades of moderately elevated blood pressure, and malignant (accelerated) nephrosclerosis, in which sudden severe hypertension (diastolic above 120 mmHg) produces fibrinoid necrosis of small renal arteries, thrombotic microangiopathy, and rapidly progressive kidney failure.

The pathological hallmarks are afferent arteriolar hyalinosis (PAS-positive amorphous material replacing smooth muscle), interlobular artery intimal thickening and fibrous hyperplasia, ischemic wrinkling and shrinkage of glomerular tufts, and tubular atrophy with interstitial fibrosis proportional to the degree of ischemia. The kidney compensates initially through adaptive hyperfiltration in remaining nephrons, which paradoxically accelerates glomerular injury. The nephrology and cardiology communities manage hypertensive nephrosclerosis jointly, given the shared cardiovascular burden. The primary debate in the field is whether hypertension truly causes nephrosclerosis in many Black patients, or whether APOL1-associated FSGS co-occurring with hypertension is misclassified as nephrosclerosis — a distinction with treatment implications.

typesClassification.description: Develops over decades of moderately elevated blood pressure (grade 1-2 hypertension). Arteriolar hyalinosis and glomerulosclerosis accumulate slowly. GFR decline is gradual — typically 1-2 mL/min/1.73m² per year. Proteinuria is mild (ACR under 300 mg/g in most cases). Mainstay of the vast majority of 'hypertensive ESRD' classification.

Occurs with diastolic blood pressure above 120 mmHg and hypertensive end-organ injury (encephalopathy, retinopathy, cardiac failure). Fibrinoid necrosis and thrombotic microangiopathy produce rapid GFR decline. Medical emergency requiring IV antihypertensive therapy. Prognosis has improved dramatically with modern treatment.

In Black Americans with two APOL1 high-risk alleles, what is clinically diagnosed as hypertensive nephrosclerosis may in fact be APOL1-associated collapsing or focal glomerulosclerosis. This distinction is increasingly recognized as it changes prognosis estimates and may alter management decisions.

primarySymptoms: Elevated blood pressure readings consistently above 130/80 mmHg in the office or on ambulatory monitoring — the upstream driver of kidney damage.
Gradual reduction in glomerular filtration rate (GFR) over years, often asymptomatic until GFR falls below 30-45 mL/min/1.73m² when uremic symptoms emerge.
Mild proteinuria — typically ACR 30-300 mg/g (microalbuminuria to mild proteinuria) — detected on urine screening, without the heavy nephrotic-range spilling of glomerulonephritis.
Nocturia — waking at night to urinate — from impaired renal concentrating ability in tubulointerstitial fibrosis; often an early functional sign preceding measurable GFR decline.
Fatigue and reduced exercise capacity from anemia of chronic kidney disease (EPO deficiency) appearing when GFR falls below 30 mL/min.
Peripheral ankle edema from sodium retention, volume expansion, and reduced GFR in established CKD stages 3-4.
Headache, visual blurring, and pulsatile tinnitus associated with uncontrolled blood pressure, which may precipitate malignant hypertension.
Decreased urine output in malignant hypertensive nephrosclerosis as microangiopathy produces rapid functional nephron loss.
Hypertensive retinopathy changes — silver-wiring, AV nicking, and in malignant hypertension flame hemorrhages and papilledema — visible on funduscopy.
earlyWarningSigns: Microalbuminuria (ACR 30-300 mg/g) on routine urine screening in a patient with established hypertension — the earliest measurable marker of hypertensive kidney injury.
Gradual rise in serum creatinine (0.1-0.2 mg/dL per year) in a hypertensive patient not explained by medication changes or dehydration.
Nocturia developing in a previously well-controlled hypertensive patient — impaired renal concentrating ability is an early sign of tubulointerstitial injury.
Funduscopic changes of grade 2-3 hypertensive retinopathy (AV nicking, focal arteriolar constriction) indicating sustained end-organ hypertensive stress.
emergencySigns: Diastolic blood pressure above 120 mmHg with confusion, visual disturbance, or neurological symptoms — hypertensive encephalopathy requiring immediate IV antihypertensive therapy (labetalol, nicardipine) and ICU admission.

Severe hypertension with bilateral papilledema (grade 4 retinopathy) and rapidly rising creatinine — malignant hypertensive nephrosclerosis requiring controlled blood pressure reduction over 24-48 hours, not abrupt normalization.

Oliguria or anuria with creatinine doubling within 48 hours in a hypertensive patient — may represent superimposed acute kidney injury, renal artery stenosis, or malignant hypertension-associated TMA.
Chest pain, dyspnea, and hypertension above 180/110 mmHg — hypertensive emergency with potential aortic dissection or acute coronary syndrome requiring emergency evaluation.

causes.description: Sustained hypertension transmits elevated pressure into the glomerular capillaries when afferent arteriolar autoregulation is overwhelmed, causing mechanical stretch-induced endothelial injury, mesangial expansion, and podocyte stress. Decades of exposure produce irreversible nephron loss.

Chronic pressure-induced endothelial injury causes plasma protein infiltration into the vessel wall, depositing hyaline material in the afferent arteriole media and displacing smooth muscle. Hyalinized arterioles cannot dilate, reducing glomerular blood flow and GFR.

Loss of nephrons from ischemia forces remaining glomeruli to hyperfiltrate. Elevated single-nephron GFR stretches the podocyte slit diaphragm, leading to foot-process effacement, protein leak, and secondary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis — perpetuating the cycle of nephron loss.

Renal ischemia from arteriolar disease activates the RAAS, generating angiotensin II, which raises systemic and intraglomerular pressure, promotes glomerulosclerosis directly, and stimulates aldosterone-driven sodium retention — creating a vicious cycle.

In Black patients with two APOL1 high-risk alleles (G1/G2), the kidney is intrinsically more susceptible to injury from any insult including hypertension. APOL1 variants independently drive podocyte apoptosis and accelerate nephrosclerosis beyond what blood pressure alone predicts.

riskFactors.description: Each decade of uncontrolled hypertension above 140/90 mmHg accelerates glomerulosclerosis. Every 10 mmHg reduction in systolic blood pressure reduces ESRD risk by approximately 17% (SPRINT, ACCORD trials).

riskFactors.factor: Black or African ancestry with APOL1 G1/G2 variants
riskFactors.description: Two APOL1 high-risk alleles increase risk of hypertension-attributed ESRD 3-5x compared to those with zero risk alleles; approximately 13% of African Americans carry both high-risk alleles.

Diabetes accelerates glomerulosclerosis independently and synergizes with hypertension to amplify nephron loss. Over 50% of ESRD cases in the US have both hypertension and diabetes listed as contributing causes.
Smoking raises blood pressure, induces endothelial dysfunction, and is an independent predictor of faster CKD progression in hypertensive patients — 2x increase in ESRD risk in heavy smokers.
Obesity raises blood pressure, increases renal venous pressure, and drives hyperfiltration-associated secondary FSGS. Weight loss of 5-10% reduces proteinuria and blood pressure.

riskFactors.factor: Poor blood pressure control or treatment non-adherence
riskFactors.description: Uncontrolled hypertension is the single most modifiable risk factor. SPRINT trial showed that targeting systolic below 120 mmHg reduced cardiovascular events and CKD progression compared to the standard target of 140 mmHg.

Aging reduces nephron number by up to 50% from baseline by age 70, leaving fewer nephrons to absorb blood pressure-related hyperfiltration stress. Age-associated arteriosclerosis amplifies hypertensive injury.

affectedDemographics: Black Americans have 3-4x higher attributed hypertensive ESRD rates than White Americans. APOL1 variants explain a significant but not complete proportion of this disparity (AASK trial data).
Adults over 50 with longstanding hypertension: hypertensive nephrosclerosis is uncommon in young adults but accelerates after age 50 as arteriolar injury accumulates.
Patients with concurrent type 2 diabetes and hypertension — the combination accounts for over 50% of US ESRD cases annually.
Low-income populations with reduced access to blood pressure monitoring and antihypertensive medication are disproportionately affected globally (WHO Hypertension Report 2023).
diagnosisOverview:
Hypertensive nephrosclerosis is largely a diagnosis of exclusion, relying on clinical context rather than pathognomonic features. The typical patient presents with longstanding hypertension (usually more than 5-10 years), gradual GFR decline, mild proteinuria (ACR under 1 g/g), hypertensive retinopathy, and left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram — consistent with other end-organ damage from sustained high blood pressure.

The initial workup quantifies kidney function with serum creatinine and CKD-EPI GFR estimate, measures proteinuria with spot ACR or 24-hour urine protein, performs urinalysis for hematuria and casts, and reviews blood pressure control over time. Laboratory tests exclude secondary causes of CKD — diabetes, glomerulonephritis (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, complement), myeloma (SPEP), and renal artery stenosis (Doppler ultrasound, CTA) — which may coexist with or mimic hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

Kidney biopsy is not routinely required but is performed when proteinuria is nephrotic-range (ACR above 1,000-3,000 mg/g), GFR decline is unexpectedly rapid, active urinary sediment suggests glomerulonephritis, or clinical features are atypical. Biopsy typically shows arteriolar hyalinosis, ischemic glomerular changes, and tubular atrophy without significant immune-complex deposits — though distinguishing advanced hypertensive nephrosclerosis from APOL1-associated FSGS can be challenging histologically. APOL1 genotyping is increasingly used in Black patients with CKD to refine prognosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies kidney function and CKD stage; tracks progression over time with serial measurements
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. eGFR calculated from creatinine, age, and sex. A stable eGFR over years despite hypertension is reassuring; progressive decline warrants further evaluation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects microalbuminuria (30-300 mg/g) as an early sign of hypertensive kidney injury; monitors proteinuria level for treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A first-morning urine sample. No special preparation. Mild proteinuria (ACR 30-300 mg/g) is typical; heavy proteinuria (above 1,000 mg/g) suggests an alternative glomerular diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures 24-hour blood pressure profile including nocturnal dipping pattern; non-dipping is associated with faster CKD progression in hypertensive nephrosclerosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood pressure cuff worn for 24 hours with automated readings every 15-30 minutes. Normal activity during the day; cuff is removed only briefly for showering.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses kidney size, echogenicity (increased in CKD), and renovascular blood flow to exclude renal artery stenosis as a secondary cause of hypertension and CKD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-minute painless ultrasound examination of the kidneys and renal vessels. Small, echogenic kidneys suggest advanced scarring consistent with hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms arteriolar hyalinosis and ischemic glomerulosclerosis; excludes alternative diagnoses such as FSGS or vasculitis when clinical presentation is atypical
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under ultrasound guidance with local anesthesia. Observation 6-8 hours afterward for bleeding. Not routinely required in classic presentations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies high-risk G1/G2 variants in Black patients to refine CKD progression risk and inform discussions about aggressive BP control, clinical trial eligibility, and transplant evaluation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory. Results in 2-4 weeks. Genetic counseling is recommended before testing.

treatmentOverview:
The primary and most evidence-supported intervention in hypertensive nephrosclerosis is aggressive blood pressure reduction. The SPRINT trial (2015) demonstrated that targeting systolic blood pressure below 120 mmHg reduced the composite cardiovascular outcome and slowed CKD progression compared with a target below 140 mmHg, though at the cost of somewhat more adverse events including acute kidney injury episodes. KDIGO 2021 CKD guidelines recommend a systolic target below 120 mmHg in most adults with CKD and hypertension, with an acceptable range down to systolic 110 mmHg if tolerated.

ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril, lisinopril) and ARBs (e.g., losartan, olmesartan) are the preferred antihypertensive classes in hypertensive nephrosclerosis with proteinuria above 300 mg/g because they reduce intraglomerular pressure independently of systemic pressure reduction and slow proteinuric CKD progression. The African American Study of Kidney Disease (AASK trial) demonstrated that ramipril significantly reduced the risk of ESRD or GFR halving compared with amlodipine or metoprolol in Black patients with hypertensive CKD — establishing ACE inhibitors as first-line therapy in this population.

For malignant hypertensive nephrosclerosis: blood pressure must be reduced by 25% within the first hour using IV labetalol or IV nicardipine, then gradually toward normal over 24-48 hours to avoid cerebral or coronary hypoperfusion. Abrupt normalization risks watershed infarcts. Once stabilized, oral ACE inhibitor-based regimens are introduced.

Additional management addresses CKD complications: anemia with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents when hemoglobin falls below 10 g/dL, mineral bone disease with vitamin D analogues and phosphate binders, metabolic acidosis with bicarbonate supplementation, and cardiovascular risk reduction with statins.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg daily or lisinopril 10-40 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antihypertensive in hypertensive nephrosclerosis with proteinuria. Reduces intraglomerular pressure via efferent arteriolar dilation, decreases proteinuria, and slows GFR decline. AASK trial demonstrated superiority over amlodipine or metoprolol in Black patients with hypertensive CKD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AASK trial: 38% reduction in primary composite endpoint (GFR halving, ESRD, or death) vs amlodipine; 22% vs metoprolol

medicalTreatments.name: ARBs (e.g., losartan 50-100 mg daily, olmesartan 20-40 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Equivalent renoprotective class to ACE inhibitors; preferred when ACE inhibitor causes intractable cough. Not combined with ACE inhibitors due to increased adverse effects without added kidney-protective benefit (ONTARGET data).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to ACE inhibitors in slowing CKD progression and reducing proteinuria in multiple trials

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine 5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Effective blood pressure-lowering agent used as add-on to ACE inhibitor or ARB. Preferred for patients who cannot tolerate RAAS blockade. Dihydropyridine CCBs are preferred over non-dihydropyridine types in proteinuric CKD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces blood pressure effectively; less renoprotective than ACE inhibitors as monotherapy in proteinuric hypertensive CKD (AASK trial data)

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin 10-25 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Originally developed for type 2 diabetes, SGLT2 inhibitors reduce intraglomerular pressure, slow CKD progression, and reduce cardiovascular events. EMPA-KIDNEY trial (2022) showed benefit in CKD with or without diabetes. Now included in KDIGO 2022 CKD updates for eGFR ≥20.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EMPA-KIDNEY: 28% reduction in kidney disease progression or cardiovascular death; benefit observed regardless of diabetic status

medicalTreatments.name: IV labetalol or nicardipine (malignant hypertension)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous agents used in hypertensive emergencies. Target 25% blood pressure reduction within 1-6 hours; further gradual reduction over 24-48 hours. Abrupt normalization risks cerebral or coronary ischemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls blood pressure rapidly in hypertensive emergencies; prevents acute organ damage from malignant nephrosclerosis progression

medicalTreatments.name: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA) + IV iron
medicalTreatments.description: Used when hemoglobin falls below 10 g/dL from anemia of CKD in hypertensive nephrosclerosis with GFR below 30. Target hemoglobin 10-11 g/dL per KDIGO anemia guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises hemoglobin by 1-2 g/dL within 4-8 weeks; reduces transfusion requirement and improves fatigue in advanced CKD

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary sodium restriction to under 2 g/day
alternativeTreatments.description: Dietary sodium reduction of this degree lowers blood pressure by 4-6 mmHg systolic in hypertensive patients, enhances the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and ARBs, and independently reduces proteinuria. Evidence-based dietary intervention endorsed by all hypertension and nephrology guidelines.

whySeeSpecialist: Nephrology referral is warranted when GFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m², proteinuria exceeds 1 g/g, CKD progression is faster than 5 mL/min/year, or there is uncertainty about the underlying diagnosis. Malignant hypertension with rapidly deteriorating kidney function is a medical emergency requiring immediate specialist evaluation.
doctorSelectionGuide: A nephrologist with experience managing hypertensive CKD and cardiovascular-renal risk, ideally affiliated with a center offering APOL1 genotyping and transplant services, is optimal. Joint management with cardiology is important given the high cardiovascular complication burden in hypertensive nephrosclerosis.
hospitalCriteria: Inpatient nephrology and intensive care capability for management of malignant hypertension
Access to kidney biopsy with electron microscopy for atypical presentations
Dialysis and kidney transplant program for advanced CKD management
keyFacilities: Nephrology outpatient clinics with CKD management programs
Academic medical centers with hypertension and CKD specialty clinics
Kidney transplant centers for patients approaching ESRD
preventionStrategies: Achieve blood pressure targets below 130/80 mmHg (or 120/80 mmHg for high-risk patients) using ACE inhibitors or ARBs as first-line agents.
Screen for microalbuminuria (ACR) annually in all patients with hypertension and treat ACE inhibitors/ARBs early, even before GFR declines.
Reduce dietary sodium to under 2 g per day — this alone lowers systolic blood pressure by 4-6 mmHg and reduces urinary albumin excretion.
Stop smoking — cessation reduces blood pressure, improves endothelial function, and halves the excess CKD progression risk from tobacco.
Maintain a healthy body weight (BMI under 25) through diet and at least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise.
Avoid NSAIDs and nephrotoxic over-the-counter medications in patients with existing hypertension and CKD.
lifestyleModifications: Monitor home blood pressure every morning and evening; keep a log to share with your clinician at each appointment.
Take antihypertensive medications consistently at the same time each day — blood pressure variability, not just average level, predicts kidney and cardiovascular outcomes.
Restrict sodium intake to under 2 g daily; read food labels and cook fresh foods when possible.
Eat 5-9 servings of fruits and vegetables daily — the DASH diet lowers systolic blood pressure by an average of 11 mmHg.
Achieve and maintain a BMI below 25 — even 5% body weight loss reduces proteinuria and systolic blood pressure meaningfully.
Exercise at least 30-45 minutes of moderate aerobic activity 5 days per week — aerobic exercise lowers systolic blood pressure by 5-8 mmHg on average.

recommended: DASH diet pattern: fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, whole grains, lean protein — lowers systolic blood pressure by 8-11 mmHg in clinical trials
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, sweet potatoes, spinach) — counteract sodium and lower blood pressure; check serum potassium if on ACE inhibitor/ARB
Moderate protein of 0.8 g/kg/day to reduce glomerular filtration pressure and proteinuria
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) — reduce blood pressure and cardiovascular risk
avoid: High-sodium processed foods (canned soups, deli meats, fast food, pickled vegetables) — each gram of excess sodium raises systolic blood pressure by approximately 1-2 mmHg
NSAIDs and high-dose aspirin — reduce renal blood flow, raise blood pressure, and worsen proteinuria in CKD
Excess protein (above 1.3 g/kg/day) — increases glomerular filtration pressure and accelerates nephron loss in CKD
Licorice products containing glycyrrhizin — raise blood pressure via mineralocorticoid-like action and worsen hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise — brisk walking, cycling, swimming, or low-impact aerobics — for 30-45 minutes five days per week is safe and beneficial in hypertensive nephrosclerosis at all CKD stages not requiring dialysis. Heavy resistance exercise that markedly raises blood pressure should be modified. Exercise programs should be guided by baseline cardiovascular fitness assessment, especially in patients over 60 or with known coronary artery disease.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed antihypertensive medications daily — never skip doses, as blood pressure rapidly rebounds and increases nephron stress.
Measure home blood pressure twice daily (morning and evening); target below 130/80 mmHg; report any readings consistently above 145/90 mmHg to your care team.
Weigh daily; a gain of more than 2 kg in 48 hours suggests fluid retention requiring medication or diet adjustment.
Record urine frequency and any changes in urine color — dark, brown, or reduced urine output warrants same-day contact with your nephrologist.
Attend laboratory tests for creatinine, potassium, and ACR every 3-6 months as guided by your nephrologist — silent progression is common.
Review medication adherence and side effects at every visit; common barriers include cost (generics are equally effective) and ACE inhibitor cough (switch to ARB).
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the degree of blood pressure control achieved, the initial severity of CKD, and genetic factors. Patients who reach and maintain systolic blood pressure below 130 mmHg with ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy typically show GFR stabilization or decline of less than 2 mL/min per year, with 10-year ESRD risk below 10% in CKD stage 3. Patients with persistent uncontrolled hypertension, heavy proteinuria, or APOL1 high-risk genotypes progress significantly faster — median time to ESRD in AASK participants not achieving blood pressure targets was 7-10 years from CKD stage 3b. Malignant hypertensive nephrosclerosis, when treated aggressively, shows partial kidney recovery in 50-60% of cases, but permanent GFR reduction is common. Cardiovascular mortality — from myocardial infarction, stroke, and heart failure — is the leading cause of death in patients with hypertensive nephrosclerosis before reaching ESRD. ESRD from hypertensive nephrosclerosis carries a 5-year survival on dialysis of approximately 35-40%.
recoveryTimeline: Benign nephrosclerosis does not reverse but can stabilize over years with optimal blood pressure control. Malignant nephrosclerosis may show partial GFR recovery over 3-6 months after blood pressure normalization. Progression to ESRD is measured in years to decades for most patients in CKD stages 1-4.
complications: End-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation — most feared long-term complication; risk is doubled by every 10 mmHg average systolic elevation above goal.

Cardiovascular disease — hypertensive left ventricular hypertrophy, coronary artery disease, and heart failure account for 50% of mortality in CKD from hypertension; statin and ACE inhibitor therapy are cornerstones.
Hypertensive retinopathy — grades 3-4 (hemorrhages, papilledema) indicate severe end-organ hypertensive injury and risk of permanent vision loss; urgent blood pressure reduction is required.
Cerebrovascular disease — hypertensive nephrosclerosis is independently associated with lacunar stroke and white-matter disease; blood pressure control reduces stroke risk by 30-40%.
Anemia of CKD — EPO deficiency in CKD stages 3-5 causes fatigue, dyspnea, and cardiovascular strain; ESA therapy is initiated when hemoglobin falls below 10 g/dL.
Mineral and bone disorder — reduced kidney activation of vitamin D and secondary hyperparathyroidism lead to bone disease, vascular calcification, and cardiovascular risk in advanced CKD.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Hypertension and Kidney Disease
supportResources.description: Patient education on how high blood pressure damages the kidneys and how to protect kidney health.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — High Blood Pressure Center
supportResources.description: Comprehensive resources on blood pressure monitoring, lifestyle modification, and medication management.

supportResources.name: KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guidelines for CKD
supportResources.description: International guidelines for chronic kidney disease management including blood pressure targets and RAAS therapy recommendations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: FSGS and hypertensive nephrosclerosis both produce CKD with proteinuria, but FSGS typically causes heavier proteinuria (over 1-3 g/g) and full nephrotic syndrome in primary forms. Biopsy in FSGS shows segmental glomerular sclerosis with podocyte injury rather than the diffuse arteriolar hyalinosis of hypertensive nephrosclerosis. APOL1-associated disease makes distinction difficult in Black patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic nephropathy causes progressive proteinuria, GFR decline, and hypertension in patients with diabetes, often mimicking hypertensive nephrosclerosis. Diabetic nephropathy shows characteristic nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions) on biopsy; hypertensive nephrosclerosis shows predominant arteriolar hyalinosis. The two diagnoses frequently co-exist in diabetic hypertensive patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal Tubular Acidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Renal tubular acidosis (RTA) causes metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap due to tubular dysfunction, presenting at an earlier CKD stage than hypertensive nephrosclerosis would typically cause acidosis. RTA is a tubular rather than vascular disorder; it does not produce hypertensive arteriolar changes on biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis is one of the top two causes of CKD and ESRD in the US; CKD classification and management applies directly to all patients with this diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: End-Stage Renal Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The endpoint of progressive hypertensive nephrosclerosis; hypertension is the listed cause of ESRD in 27-29% of new US cases annually.

relatedConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares APOL1 genetic risk in Black patients; often a pathological co-diagnosis with hypertensive nephrosclerosis on biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent co-diagnosis; both are managed with ACE inhibitors and blood pressure reduction; distinction is by biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Heavy proteinuria from secondary FSGS or misclassified APOL1-associated disease can mimic nephrotic syndrome in hypertensive patients; biopsy clarifies.

faqs.question: What is hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis is kidney damage caused by long-standing high blood pressure. The sustained pressure scars small kidney blood vessels and glomeruli, reducing the kidney's ability to filter blood. It is the second most common cause of kidney failure in the United States after diabetes.

faqs.question: What causes hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Chronic high blood pressure hardens and narrows the small arteries feeding the kidney's filtering units (glomeruli), causing ischemia and scarring. Overactivation of the renin-angiotensin system amplifies the injury. APOL1 gene variants significantly accelerate kidney damage in Black patients with hypertension.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Early stages are usually asymptomatic. Later symptoms include mild protein in the urine (foamy urine), nocturia, fatigue from anemia, ankle swelling, and high blood pressure. Kidney function declines gradually over years. Malignant hypertension can cause rapid kidney failure with headache and visual disturbance.

faqs.question: How is hypertensive nephrosclerosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical in most cases: long-standing hypertension, gradual GFR decline, mild to moderate proteinuria, and absence of other glomerular diseases. Urine ACR, serum creatinine, kidney ultrasound, and ambulatory blood pressure monitoring are standard tests. Kidney biopsy is reserved for atypical presentations.

faqs.question: Can hypertensive nephrosclerosis be reversed?
faqs.answer: Established scarring cannot be reversed, but progression can be significantly slowed or halted. Achieving blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg with ACE inhibitors or ARBs stabilizes GFR in most patients. The AASK trial showed ramipril reduced kidney failure risk by 38% compared to other agents in Black patients with hypertensive CKD.

faqs.question: What is the best blood pressure target to protect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: KDIGO 2021 and the SPRINT trial recommend systolic blood pressure below 120 mmHg for most CKD patients with hypertension. A less stringent target below 130/80 mmHg is acceptable for older patients or those with poor tolerance. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred agents because they also reduce intraglomerular pressure.

faqs.question: Will hypertensive nephrosclerosis lead to kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily with good blood pressure control. Patients who achieve systolic blood pressure below 130 mmHg typically have GFR decline of less than 2 mL/min per year and a 10-year ESRD risk below 10% in CKD stage 3. However, uncontrolled hypertension with APOL1 high-risk variants carries a significantly higher ESRD risk.

faqs.question: Why are Black Americans more affected by hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Black Americans have 3-4x higher rates of ESRD attributed to hypertension. A key factor is the APOL1 gene — approximately 13% of Black Americans carry two high-risk variants (G1 or G2) that make kidney cells more vulnerable to injury from hypertension. Disparities in blood pressure control and healthcare access also contribute.

faqs.question: What medications treat hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: ACE inhibitors (ramipril, lisinopril) or ARBs (losartan, olmesartan) are first-line, as they lower blood pressure and directly protect the kidney. SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) are now added in CKD patients for additional kidney and heart protection. Calcium channel blockers and diuretics control blood pressure as add-on therapy.

faqs.question: What is malignant hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Malignant nephrosclerosis occurs when blood pressure rises acutely to very high levels (diastolic above 120 mmHg) causing fibrinoid necrosis and thrombotic microangiopathy in renal arterioles. It presents with rapid kidney failure, papilledema, and encephalopathy. Emergency IV blood pressure lowering is required, but recovery of kidney function is incomplete in most cases.

faqs.question: Does salt intake affect hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. A diet high in sodium raises blood pressure and worsens kidney injury. Reducing sodium intake to under 2 g per day lowers systolic blood pressure by 4-6 mmHg and reduces urinary albumin excretion. The DASH diet, which is low in sodium and high in potassium, lowers blood pressure by 8-11 mmHg in clinical trials.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Prognosis depends on blood pressure control and genetic risk. Patients who reach blood pressure targets with ACE inhibitors typically stabilize at CKD stage 3 for many years. Those with uncontrolled hypertension, heavy proteinuria, or APOL1 high-risk variants progress more rapidly to ESRD over 7-15 years from diagnosis.

faqs.question: How does kidney biopsy help in hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Biopsy is not needed in classic presentations. It is used when proteinuria is heavy (suggesting FSGS or glomerulonephritis), GFR declines faster than expected, or active urinary sediment suggests inflammation. Biopsy shows arteriolar hyalinosis and ischemic glomeruli in hypertensive nephrosclerosis, distinguishing it from other causes.

faqs.question: Can exercise help hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Moderate aerobic exercise — 30-45 minutes five days per week — lowers blood pressure by 5-8 mmHg, reduces cardiovascular risk, and is safe in CKD stages 1-4. Exercise should be moderate intensity (walking, cycling, swimming); heavy lifting that acutely raises blood pressure should be modified.

faqs.question: Can SGLT2 inhibitors help hypertensive nephrosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. SGLT2 inhibitors such as empagliflozin and dapagliflozin reduce intraglomerular pressure, slow CKD progression, and reduce cardiovascular events in patients with CKD regardless of diabetic status. The EMPA-KIDNEY trial showed a 28% reduction in kidney disease progression, and KDIGO 2022 recommends them for eGFR at or above 20.

metaDescription: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis: kidney scarring from high blood pressure, a leading ESRD cause. AASK findings, ACE inhibitor treatment, and prognosis reviewed.

sources.title: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO). 2021. KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases. Kidney Int 100(4S):S1-S276.
Appel LJ, Wright JT Jr, Greene T, et al. 2010. Intensive blood-pressure control in hypertensive chronic kidney disease. N Engl J Med 363(10):918-29. (AASK trial)
SPRINT Research Group. 2015. A randomized trial of intensive versus standard blood-pressure control. N Engl J Med 373(22):2103-16.
